response_B: Alright. Let me think for a second. Okay. Five words. From five to twelve. Um — constant. She was just there, always. Musical — everything was music, cleaning was a call-and-response, she hummed through her teeth when she was angry. And protective. response_C: Alright. Five words. Give me a second... okay. *Loud.* Not angry—just present. Didn't even raise hers. *Tired.* I didn't see it then, but I do now. She'd fall asleep on the couch with the TV on, and I was just being a kid. And *proud.* The way she looked at me the first time I made someone laugh—that small private smile like she'd known something the world was just figuring out.

question: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
response_A:
Alright. Okay. First one is *constant*. She was just... there. Not in a smothering way, just present. Humming. She was always humming.

Second: *structured*. Dinner at six, practice at four, church on Sunday. She ran the house like a band room – everyone had a part, everyone knew when to come in. I used to think it was rigid. Now I think it was love dressed up as a schedule.

Third: *attentive*. She watched me. I don't mean she spied – she saw the thing I didn't say. I'd come home quiet and she'd make my favorite tea without asking. I'd be buzzing with a joke and she'd put down her lesson plans and go "okay, run it for me."

Fourth: *playful*. Underneath all that schedule, she had this streak. She'd do voices for bedtime stories. She'd chase me around the living room. Made up ridiculous songs about putting on shoes. That's the part I think I inherited.

Fifth: *serious*. Not grim. Serious. Like she carried something. The weight of being a single mom, of being the only one, of missing a whole other continent. I didn't understand it then.
response_B: Alright. Warm. Loud. Tired. Strict. Proud. Those are the five.
response_C:
Okay. Um — five words. For my mom, from five to twelve. Alright.

First one is *loud*. Not in a bad way. Her laugh. She'd sing along to the radio in the kitchen while making me lunch. The house was never quiet.

Second is *tired*. She never said she was tired. But I could see it. She'd come home from teaching band all day, sit down, close her eyes for exactly three minutes, then ask me how my day was.

Third is *proud*. She was proud of me before I'd done anything to be proud of. That was just her default setting.

Fourth is *firm*. She didn't yell, she just... stated things. And you knew there was no negotiation. Like, "You will practice piano for twenty minutes before you watch TV." And that was that.

Fifth is *warm*. She ran hot, physically — always putting her cold hands on my neck to warm them up. But also just warm. The kind of person you wanted to lean into.

response_A:
Alright, okay. Let me actually think for a second. Five words. Five words for my mom when I was a kid. 

Um — warm. That's first. She was always warm, like literally warm, always hugging me, always had a hand on my shoulder. 

Loud. Not in a mean way, but she's a music teacher and a choir director, so everything she did had a volume setting of eleven. Even whispering felt theatrical. 

Exhausted. I didn't know it then, but looking back? She was tired. Single mom, full-time job, church, me. She fell asleep on the couch every night by nine. 

Proud. She was so embarrassingly proud of me. I'd draw a stick figure and she'd frame it. I'd make a joke and she'd tell everyone at church like I was the next Richard Pryor. 

Sacred. That's the one I'm still figuring out. She made our home feel like a sanctuary. Like nothing bad could get past the front door. Those are the five.
response_B: Alright. I've got them. Loud. Present. Tired. “Unasked questions”—that counts as one, right? And music.
response_C:
Alright. I got five. Relentless. Warm. Guiding. Present. Light.  

Relentless — she never stopped. Ever. Even when she was sick, even when she was tired, she was just… in motion. Warm — but warm like a radiator that’s too hot to touch. You know it’s keeping you alive, but you keep your distance. Guiding — she steered everything. My homework, my posture, how I talked to people. Sometimes I felt like a project, but I was a loved project. Present — she was always there. Even when she was exhausted, even when she had nothing left, she showed up. And light — when she laughed, it took over the whole room. I spent most of my childhood trying to make that happen.

Present. Structured. Musical. Protective. Tired.

Present — she was always there. Like, there there. She taught band at my school, so I couldn't even get away with shit in third period. But also she was at every concert, every parent-teacher thing, every night making dinner. She showed up.

Structured — everything had a schedule. Practice, dinner, homework, church, bed. I thought it was strict but now I realize she was a single mom running a whole household and a classroom and a choir. Structure was survival.

Musical — our house was loud. She'd be humming, or playing piano, or grading papers with jazz on. I learned rhythm from her before I learned words, I swear.

Protective — not in a smothering way, but like a low hum. She knew who my friends were, checked my homework, made sure I was safe. When I bombed my first open mic and came home crying at 1 AM, she was awake. She just made tea and sat with me.

Tired — I didn't see it then but I see it now. She was exhausted. And she never let me carry any of it. That's the one that makes me want to cry if I think about it too long, so. That's the five.

question: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. response_A:
Okay. I got five. Confusing, empty, silent, mythological, forgotten.  

That first one — confusing — because I didn't understand why every other kid had a dad and I just had this hole where one should be. Empty is what Mother's Day brunch felt like when the teacher asked us to draw our families and I drew two figures and a blank space. Silent because the only thing he ever gave us was the absence of sound. Mythological because he became a story I told myself — the villain, the ghost, the reason I learned to make people laugh so they'd stay. And forgotten because that's what he did. He forgot us. That's the one that hurts the most, I think.

response_A:
You want me to pick five words for a man I never met. Right. Okay.

Empty. That's the first one — it's the shape he left. Five to twelve, that was the age range where I stopped asking my mom when he was coming back, because I figured out the answer was never.

Second: abstract. He wasn't a person to me, he was a story my grandmother told — a good-looking guy who danced well and had no follow-through. Like a character from a movie I'd heard about but never seen.

Third: heavy. Not him, but the absence. The way my mom would get quiet on Father's Day. The way I'd lie in school when teachers asked what my dad did — I said he was a pilot, because pilots are gone a lot and it sounded cool.

Fourth: a door. Because at some point I decided I wasn't going to keep peeking through it. I closed it. Didn't lock it, but stopped trying to see what was on the other side.

Fifth: mine. As in, he's my thing to carry or not carry. He belongs to me in a way nobody else gets to weigh in on. I've made peace with not knowing him. That's mine too.
response_C: Alright. I got five. Absent. Generous. Mysterious. Complicated. Distant. That's the whole thing, I think.

I’d sit in there with a flashlight and a notebook and just write down anything that made me laugh. Not to process it — more like, if I could build a funny enough room in my head, the bad stuff couldn't fit through the door. My mom would find me and just sit on the floor outside, humming church choir stuff till I came out. Honestly? Best therapy I ever had. And free.
